title: Polymer clay sculpted snail
subtitle: A small coiled snail figurine in polymer clay
excerpt: A small polymer clay garden snail built from a body shape and a separate coiled shell. The spiral shell is built up from a thick coil of clay; the body and head are sculpted separately and attached before curing. About 50 mm long when complete.
sourceNotes: Polymer clay miniature figurines technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A PVC-based modelling material that cures permanently in a domestic oven at 110-130°C.
Oven-baking polymer clay to harden it.

content.text: A
polymer clay

snail is an ideal first figurine project: the body is a simple elongated shape and the shell is just a thick coil wound into a spiral. Use two colours to distinguish shell from body: brown, tan, or cream for the shell; pale yellow-green or grey for the body.

attrs.content: Use a dedicated oven tray. Do not share with food-prep cookware. Cure at manufacturer's stated temperature.

items.name: Polymer clay, pale green or grey (body)

content.text: Steps
Roll a tapered brown clay coil and wind it into a spiral to form the shell.
Shape the body from pale clay as a teardrop with a curved tail.
Press the shell onto the back of the body and add eye and antenna details.
Cure on a dedicated tray at 130°C for 20 minutes and cool before handling.
Building the shell

Condition 20 g of brown clay and roll it into a tapered coil: start with a 6 mm thick rope, then taper one end to a point over the last 30 mm. Wind the thick end into a flat spiral, pressing each coil firmly against the one below. The spiral should be 35 mm across and 20 mm tall. The tapered end finishes at the centre top of the spiral, pointing upward slightly. Smooth the outer face with a finger.
Building the body

Condition 15 g of pale clay and form it into a teardrop shape 50 mm long and 15 mm wide at the widest point. Slightly flatten the underside so the body lies stable. Curve the body slightly upward at the front (head) end.
Attaching and detailing

Press the shell down onto the back half of the body, pressing firmly. The flat base of the shell should contact the top of the body over a 15 mm area. Add two tiny balls of black clay for eyes at the front of the head. Add two thin antenna stalks using the needle tool to mark shallow channels from each eye extending 10 mm back and slightly outward. Score the shell surface lightly with the needle tool in a spiral pattern to suggest individual coil seams.
Curing
Preheat the oven to 130°C. Place the snail on a dedicated lined tray.
Cure
for 20 minutes. Cool on the tray before handling.
Variations
Add two short antenna tentacles before curing using thin rolls of the body colour clay.
Make a family of three snails in graduated sizes to arrange as a table display.
Use a marbled two-colour shell for a more decorative look: twist two brown shades together before rolling the shell coil.

items.symptom: Shell lifts away from the body after curing
items.cause: Shell was not pressed firmly enough onto the body before curing
items.fix: Press the shell and body together and add a tiny rolled coil of clay around the join base, smoothing it flush, before curing

items.symptom: Spiral unwinds during handling before curing
items.cause: Clay not conditioned enough; too stiff to hold the spiral shape
items.fix: Knead the brown clay more thoroughly. Chill the built spiral briefly in the freezer for five minutes to stiffen it if it continues to unwind
